title: Seasonal and sexual variations in plasma concentrations of testosterone, 17β-estradiol, and progesterone in the endangered Beale's Eyed Turtle, Sacalia bealei, in captivity.
authors: Hui Li, Han Yang, Ying-Nan Lu, Hai-Tao Shi, Liu Lin
summary: Scientists studied how hormone levels change throughout the year in a critically endangered Chinese turtle species kept in captivity. They found that male and female turtles have different seasonal patterns of three key reproductive hormones—testosterone, estradiol, and progesterone—that control when they breed and reproduce.
key_points: Male turtles show two peaks of testosterone (in May and August), suggesting they prepare for mating twice a year using a special strategy where they produce sperm after the breeding season ends
Female turtles show a single peak of estradiol in April that triggers egg development, with progesterone peaking in June during ovulation, indicating they breed once per year
The hormone patterns are interconnected and work together to coordinate all the reproductive processes in both sexes
why_it_matters: Understanding these natural hormone cycles is essential for breeding programs and conservation efforts aimed at saving this critically endangered turtle species from extinction.
